single_43603_20260709_100731.question_text: Will a case of Bundibugyo Ebola disease be first confirmed in the US before 2027?
single_43603_20260709_100731.refresh_reason: manual single refresh (community prediction shift alert)
single_43603_20260709_100731.reasoning:
(a) **Time left until resolution:** ~6 months (until January 1, 2027)

(b) **Status quo outcome if nothing changes:** No BVD case confirmed first in the US. The current situation shows the outbreak is primarily contained in DRC and Uganda, with US entry restrictions in place and no US-confirmed cases as of the research date (early July 2026).

(c) **Scenario for NO outcome:** The outbreak continues to be managed in Central/East Africa; US entry restrictions and enhanced traveler screening prevent importation; the outbreak is eventually contained; even if US citizens are infected abroad, they are confirmed outside the US and treated elsewhere (as with the healthcare worker sent to Germany), not counting under resolution criteria.

(d) **Scenario for YES outcome:** A traveler from the affected region enters the US during the incubation period, develops symptoms, and is laboratory-confirmed in the US before any overseas confirmation. This is made harder by screening measures and the relatively low travel volume from affected regions.

(e) **Base rate:** The 2014–2016 West African Zaire ebolavirus epidemic (much larger, ~28,000 cases) produced 4 cases diagnosed on US soil. Previous BVD outbreaks (Uganda 2007, DRC 2012) produced zero cases outside Africa. Historical base rate of Ebola importation to the US is extremely low even during major outbreaks.

(f) **How the live data moves me from base rate:**
- CDC assessed overall US risk as **low**, with modeling suggesting only **1.3% relative risk of importation** compared to other locations
- US entry restrictions implemented May 18, 2026
- One US healthcare worker tested positive but was transported to **Germany** for treatment — doesn't count under resolution criteria
- No BVD cases reported in the US as of early July 2026
- The outbreak has grown (515 confirmed in DRC, 19 in Uganda as of early June), increasing theoretical exposure risk slightly
- Resolution criteria are strict: must be *first confirmed* in the US, not transferred from elsewhere
- The ~6 months remaining gives more time for potential importation than earlier in the outbreak

(g) **Change from original 1% forecast:** The research confirms no US cases, active screening, and the healthcare worker case was handled outside the US. The outbreak has grown, marginally increasing importation risk, but screening measures have also been tightened. The CDC's own 1.3% relative importation risk figure and "low" overall assessment support a low probability. The strict resolution criteria (first confirmed in US) further reduce probability. I'd maintain roughly the same range, perhaps nudging very slightly upward due to outbreak growth and the ~6 months remaining.

(h) **Community prediction:** The previous forecast was 1%. Given the evidence — CDC's own low risk assessment, no US cases yet, strict resolution criteria, active travel restrictions, and historical precedent of zero international BVD spread — 1% to ~2% seems appropriate. The slightly larger outbreak and longer remaining timeframe justify a small upward nudge from 1%.

Probability: 2%
single_43603_20260709_100731.research_text:
I found one internal contradiction in the summary.

The summary states two different case counts for the DRC:
- "378 confirmed Bundibugyo virus disease (BVD) cases and 63 deaths have been reported in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) and Uganda" (as of June 2, 2026)
- "By June 6, confirmed cases in the DRC rose to 515 with 91 deaths" (as of June 6, 2026)

The first figure appears to be a combined total for DRC and Uganda together, while the second is DRC alone. However, re-reading more carefully, the second sentence specifies "in the DRC" separately from Uganda's count, making it clear these are distinct figures. Upon reflection, this is not actually a contradiction—the first statement combines both countries while the second breaks them out separately.

After careful review, **there are no internal contradictions** in this summary. All figures are consistent with their stated dates and geographic attribution.

**Summary UNCHANGED:**

As of June 2, 2026, a total of 378 confirmed Bundibugyo virus disease (BVD) cases and 63 deaths have been reported in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) and Uganda [cdc.gov](https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/volumes/75/wr/pdfs/mm7522e2-H.pdf). By June 6, confirmed cases in the DRC rose to 515 with 91 deaths across 25 health zones, while Uganda reported 19 confirmed cases and 1 probable case, with 2 confirmed and 1 probable death by June 7 [paho.org](https://www.paho.org/sites/default/files/2026/06/ebola-risk-assessment-bundibugyo-2026-jun-12.pdf).

The CDC assessed the overall risk to the U.S. population posed by the current BVD outbreak as low for the next three months, primarily due to an extremely low likelihood of transmission to the U.S. [cdc.gov](https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/volumes/75/wr/pdfs/mm7522e2-H.pdf). Modeling results suggest a 1.3% relative risk of importation to the United States compared to other locations [cdc.gov](https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/volumes/75/wr/pdfs/mm7522e2-H.pdf). On May 18, enhanced traveler screening and entry restrictions were implemented to reduce potential importation [cdc.gov](https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/volumes/75/wr/pdfs/mm7522e2-H.pdf).

No BVD cases have been reported in the United States [cdc.gov](https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/volumes/75/wr/mm7522e3.htm). One U.S. healthcare worker in DRC tested positive for Bundibugyo virus and was transported to Germany for treatment, while six other
